title: Certified spectral functions from lattice Monte Carlo data
abstract: The Monte Carlo method, applied to lattice quantum field theory, gives access to Euclidean correlation functions with well-understood error bars. Recovering the observables one cares about, such as the spectral density, requires solving an ill-posed inverse problem, usually tackled with heuristics that lose rigorous control of the error. Instead of trying to find the ``best'' spectral density $ρ(ω)$, we ask how small or large linear functionals $\int_{\mathbb{R}^+} G(ω) ρ(ω) \mathrm{d} ω$ of it can be, given the Monte Carlo data and the reflection positivity of the lattice action. This is a convex but infinite-dimensional problem. We show how its dual can be rigorously relaxed into a hierarchy of finite semidefinite programs, solvable with standard solvers and enjoying strong convergence guarantees. The resulting bounds are rigorous even when the relaxation is not tight, and converge quickly to the regime where the error is entirely dominated by Monte Carlo statistics. The method also flags implausible Monte Carlo data, for instance underestimated error bars, through an infeasibility certificate. We demonstrate it on lattice $φ^4$ theory in two dimensions.
summary: This paper presents a rigorous mathematical framework to extract spectral information ($\rho(\omega)$) from noisy lattice Monte Carlo measurements. It transforms the difficult inverse problem into a sequence of solvable Semidefinite Programs (SDPs) by constructing bounding envelopes for the underlying kernel functions. This yields mathematically guaranteed upper and lower bounds on physical observables, providing both quantitative results and a powerful consistency check for the input data.
main_problem: The core challenge is rigorously recovering spectral observables, such as the spectral density $\rho(\omega)$, from Euclidean correlation functions measured via lattice Monte Carlo simulations.
main_result: The authors developed a method to derive rigorous upper and lower bounds on arbitrary linear functionals of $\rho(\omega)$ by relaxing the problem into solvable Semidefinite Programs (SDPs).
method: The approach reformulates the ill-posed inverse problem as a convex optimization problem, which is then rigorously relaxed into a hierarchy of finite SDPs using bounding envelopes for the kernel function.
model_or_system: The work applies to lattice quantum field theory, demonstrated specifically on two-dimensional $\phi^4$ theory. The analysis relies on relating measured correlation functions $C(t)$ to the spectral density via a lattice Källén-Lehmann representation.
key_observables: Spectral density $\rho(\omega)$, Euclidean correlation functions $C(t)$, and linear functionals of $\rho(\omega)$.
important_parameters: The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ derived from Monte Carlo data, the maximum allowed deviation $F_{\max}$, and the lattice spacing $a$.
main_assumptions: Assumes that the underlying physics is governed by a reflection positive action and that statistical errors are approximately Gaussian-distributed. The method relies on constructing bounding envelopes for continuous functions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the convergence of bounds using piecewise constant versus piecewise affine basis sets, showing how these envelopes provide rigorous upper and lower bounds on the spectral density as the number of basis functions increases.
paper_structure: The paper builds from identifying the ill-posed nature of the problem to formulating it as a convex optimization problem. It then details the mathematical machinery of Lagrange duality and relaxation techniques, culminating in solving the resulting finite SDPs for rigorous bounds.
why_interesting: While focused on lattice QFT, the methodology—transforming an infinite-dimensional inverse problem into a sequence of solvable, rigorously bounded SDPs—is highly relevant to any field involving ill-posed reconstruction from noisy data (e.g., spectral analysis in quantum dynamics).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Middle: Schematic projection of the convex space of admissible positive spectral densities. Left: Approximation by discretizing in ω, which is fine for all practical purposes, if a large number of variables Nv for discretization in ω is taken. The resulting bounds approach the true ones from inside as the number of grid points Nv grows, but are not rigorous at finite Nv (orange). Right: Rigorously bounding the K¨all´en-Lehmann kernel by piecewise constant (turquoise) or piecewise affine (dark blue) envelopes enlarges the feasible set. The resulting bounds approach the true ones from outside as the number of envelope pieces grows, and are rigorous at every finite Nv.

FIG. 2. Rigorous piecewise constant (turquoise) and piece- wise affine (dark blue) upper and lower envelopes f ± j (ω) for the lattice kernel (6) of the K¨all´en-Lehmann relation at one representative tj. Convexity of K(ω, tj) on (0, ∞) ensures that secants provide rigorous upper and tangents provide rig- orous lower bounds.

FIG. 3. Convergence with the number of constant or affine basis functions Nv for the envelopes f ± of the kernel K(ω, t). We used 104 configurations for ϕ4-theory at λ = 4 · 10−3 and g = 2, on a 256 × 256 lattice. We also show the bounds obtained by solving the primal SDP (12) discretized on a grid with Nv discretization points.

FIG. 4. Rigorous upper and lower bounds on the smeared spectral density (σ2 = 0.2) from the dual SDP (26) for ϕ4- theory at λ = 4 · 10−3 and g = 2, on a 256 × 256 lattice. The symmetrized correlator (4) provides Nc = 129 constraints. The vertical dashed line corresponds to the mass-gap esti- mate, obtained from an effective-mass fit.
